Apptio was acquired by IBM during Marwan's time there. As the organization integrated, it shifted toward more process overhead and company-wide structural changes. He's looking for a role with more direct technical ownership.

q: what are you looking for next
a: Marwan is looking for a senior role where he can own systems end to end — architecture and platform work on data-intensive, cloud-native products. He works best with direct technical ownership and a high bar, and brings 15+ years of doing exactly that at HP, JumpCloud, and Apptio.

q: what is he most proud of
tell me about an accomplishment you're especially proud of
a: Marwan planned and executed the scaling of HP's Instant Ink subscription service from thousands to millions of customers, globally. He transformed a Ruby on Rails monolith into cloud-native microservices and data pipelines using Domain-Driven Design and Test-Driven Development — completed in a matter of months, across 4 geographically distributed teams, with service reliability maintained throughout.

q: what project challenged you the most, and how did you approach it
a: At Apptio, Marwan migrated Kubernetes and Java workloads to EMR Spark with Scala to solve a scaling and cost problem — pipelines were running for days and getting expensive. The hard part was correctness: financial datasets have zero tolerance for drift. He built a reconciliation pipeline that ran both systems in parallel and flagged discrepancies, which gave the team confidence to cut over incrementally rather than all at once. The result was roughly 10x faster processing with zero correctness drift.

q: how do you approach platform architecture
a: Platform architecture is the core of Marwan's work. At HP he decomposed a Rails monolith into cloud-native microservices on AWS using Domain-Driven Design. At JumpCloud he architected a hybrid Postgres and MongoDB data layer for a multi-tenant identity platform serving 50,000+ tenants. At Apptio he designed and migrated data-processing pipelines for high-volume financial datasets. The through-line: clear service boundaries, operable systems, and designs that scale with both data and teams.
